title: Whakairo Awa: Carving the River's Story / Te Whakairo o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
subject: Arts
culturalContext: Students will explore the art of whakairo (carving) through the story of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (The Mangakōtukutuku Stream), a significant waterway that flows through our rohe. They will learn how traditional carving patterns (kōwhaiwhai) tell the story of the awa's journey from the mountains to the sea, its significance to Ngāti Kahungunu, and our role as kaitiaki in preserving its mauri (life force).
learningObjectives: Analyze and interpret traditional kōwhaiwhai patterns that represent elements of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, identifying at least three symbolic connections to the local environment.
Create an original whakairo design on wood or clay that visually narrates a specific segment of the awa's story, incorporating at least two traditional patterns and one contemporary element.
Present and justify artistic choices in a digital portfolio, explaining how the design reflects both personal connection and cultural understanding of kaitiakitanga for the awa.
Collaborate effectively in a small group to provide constructive feedback on peers' designs using established cultural and artistic criteria.

activities.title: He Hīkoi Awa: Walking the River's Path
activities.description: Guided hīkoi (walk) along a restored section of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku with a local kaumātua. Students sketch and photograph natural forms, flow patterns, and significant sites, focusing on visual elements that could inspire carving designs. They record the kōrero (stories) shared about the awa's history and significance.

activities.title: Ngā Kōrero o ngā Kōwhaiwhai: Decoding the Patterns
activities.description: Hands-on workshop with a local carver or weaver. Students examine and replicate traditional kōwhaiwhai patterns (e.g., puhoro for speed, mangōpare for strength) using grid paper and digital tablets. They then match these patterns to the specific characteristics and stories of their section of the awa documented during the hīkoi.

activities.title: Te Whakairo: From Papatūānuku to Taonga
activities.description: Practical carving session. Using locally sourced clay or reclaimed wood, students transfer their designed patterns onto a physical medium. They learn and apply basic whakairo techniques under expert guidance, focusing on the connection between the material (a gift from Papatūānuku) and the story they are telling.

activities.title: Te Whakanui i te Taonga: Presenting the Treasure
activities.description: Students create a digital portfolio entry for their finished piece. This includes high-quality images, an artist's statement explaining the story and symbols used, and a short audio reflection on what the process taught them about their role as a kaitiaki. They prepare to present this to their peers.

resources: Local kaumātua and carver from Ngāti Kahungunu (e.g., expert from the local marae)
Physical and digital access to the collection at MTG Hawke's Bay, specifically taonga featuring kōwhaiwhai
Digital tablets with Procreate or Adobe Fresco for pattern design
Reclaimed wood from local sources (e.g., fallen branches from native trees) or air-dry clay

tasks: Completion of a finished whakairo piece (clay or wood) that visually narrates a story of the awa
A digital portfolio entry containing images of the work-in-progress and final piece
An artist's statement (written or recorded) that explains the cultural narrative and symbolic choices

nzcAlignment: Understanding the Arts in Context: UC – Research the work of Māori carvers and the cultural narratives they express.
Developing Practical Knowledge: PK – Explore and use art-making conventions, applying knowledge of elements and principles. Select and use appropriate materials, tools, and techniques.
Developing Ideas: DI – Generate, develop, and refine visual ideas in response to a variety of motivations, observation, and imagination, supported by the study of artists’ works.
Communicating and Interpreting: CI – Describe and evaluate how ideas and art-making processes are used to communicate meaning in their own and others’ work.
